meta.title: Family Office as a Service — Playable Case Study
meta.hubTagline: The conflict-free, flat-fee admin layer for India's newly-liquid — reporting, bill-pay, tax, succession and concierge, agnostic to who manages the money.

hero.sub: A playable walk-through of the Family-Office-as-a-Service dossier. Same facts, same numbers — but you get to investigate, decide, and vote. This one is Aamara's own backyard.

opening.lead: India is minting new liquid wealth faster than almost any economy on earth — but the plumbing to administer that wealth is a decade behind the money. The whole thesis is being paid not to touch the money.

stats.labelHtml: UHNWI (US$30M+) in India, early 2026 — 6th-largest globally, up 63% since 2021 (Knight Frank)
Family offices in India in 2024, up from 45 in 2018 — a ~7x lag behind the wealth (PwC/EY)
Bank relationship managers who admitted mis-selling products to hit targets (Oct-2024 survey)
Wealth-management asset pool by FY29, more than doubling from ~$1.1T in FY24

imgband.title: Three unsatisfying choices
imgband.sub: Product-push private banks that earn 65–70% commission on what they sell you · % AUM multi-family offices that chase ₹100 cr+ and find flat fees uneconomic · software-only tools that won't chase your CA or organise your will. The middle is empty.

whitespace.lead: Nine real players, plotted. Hover any dot. Notice where nobody is: conflict-free flat-fee and admin-first, agnostic to who manages the money. That corner is what this business is built to own.

players.d: ₹14,000 cr AUM, competes for the money
reporting software — a tool, not a service

whitespace.sidebarHtml:
Reading the map.

The big incumbents — 360 ONE, Nuvama, ASK, Kotak — are % AUM and distribution-led, and chase ₹100 cr+. The fee-only purists Waterfield & Entrust validate the conflict-free thesis but stay advisory-first for the top UHNI. Asset Vantage is admin-shaped — but it is software, not a service; it won't do your bill-pay or chase your CA.

The top-right — a conflict-free, flat-fee human admin layer for the ₹25–200 cr band — is uncontested. That is the investable idea.

hook.lead: The whole design hinges on one line: this entity is deliberately not a SEBI Registered Investment Adviser. Pure administration is uncapped; securities advice is capped at ₹1.51 lakh and must be referred out. Click each case file to see which side of the line it falls on — and why the partition is the moat.
hook.statusInitialTail: open them all to close the case.
hook.statusSolvedHtml: Case closed. Reporting, bill-pay, vault and adviser-coordination stay in-house at an uncapped flat retainer — because none of them is 'investment advice in securities.' Recommending a security or managing the money crosses the SEBI RIA line (₹1.51 lakh cap) and is referred out. That partition is not a loophole — it is the conflict-free moat.

cases.name: Consolidated reporting
cases.det: Factual cross-asset consolidation is pure administration, not securities advice. Uncapped flat fee applies — and it is the client's first 'wow', a single view they've never had before.

cases.name: Bill-pay & document vault
cases.clue: “Run the plumbing of my financial life.”
cases.det: Bookkeeping, bill-pay and encrypted custody are admin. They are also the switching cost — once your data and rails live here, leaving means re-plumbing an entire financial life.

cases.name: Coordinating your CA & lawyer
cases.det: 'Advice-coordination' means project-managing the client's own CA, lawyer and RIA — it is not giving advice. The language discipline must be rigorous in every contract, or the RIA line blurs.

cases.name: Recommending which fund to buy
cases.det: That is investment advice in securities — SEBI RIA territory, ₹1.51 lakh fixed-fee cap. Referred out to the client's own RIA/PMS, or run through a separately-registered, separately-capped RIA subsidiary.

cases.name: Managing the money
cases.clue: “You hold it all — just run it too.”
cases.det: Discretionary money management would forfeit the conflict-free moat entirely. It is a deliberate, permanent line — not a stepping stone. Refer to vetted RIAs/PMS and stay the neutral quarterback.

personas.lead: Every persona is captured at a moment of need — an exit, an ESOP buyback, an inheritance, a return from abroad. Flip each card to see the trigger, the objection, and what converts them.

items.name: Arjun, 39
items.trig: Just sold or secondary'd his SaaS company — ~₹80 cr in the bank for the first time; his VC or i-banker at the exit is the warm intro.
items.obj: “I have money now and no system.” Cash-rich, time-poor, unsophisticated on admin, and wary of mis-selling banks.
items.conv: One trusted quarterback, a single consolidated view, conflict-free advice and speed — the flat fee is trivial against the sum being administered.

items.name: Priya, 44
items.trig: Concentrated US-listed RSUs plus Indian ESOPs; ₹30–60 cr, most of it in one stock; captured at an employer ESOP-liquidity event.
items.obj: Fragmentation, cross-border tax, single-stock concentration risk, no consolidated multi-currency view.
items.conv: Consolidated reporting + tax coordination + a diversification plan executed via her own advisers — she values the tax-complexity relief most.

items.name: The Mehta Heir, 31
items.trig: Inheriting ₹100–200 cr of mixed assets; one of the 93% who does not want to run the family business.
items.obj: Wants governance, next-gen education and a clean succession structure — stewardship without becoming a CFO.
items.conv: A multi-decade relationship: next-gen education, a succession framework, and one accountable contact — durable and high willingness to pay.

items.name: Mr. & Mrs. Rao
items.trig: Returning from the US/UK/Singapore with ₹50–150 cr across global and Indian assets; found via NRI-return and cross-border-tax networks.
items.obj: Two financial lives, FEMA, cross-border tax, multi-currency reporting, no single view.
items.conv: Consolidated multi-currency reporting + FEMA coordination + a single accountable contact across time zones — the Premier tier fits.

tycoon.lead: Four real decisions from the dossier. Each has a right answer the diligence already argues for. Pick — then see what the evidence says.

decisions.ctx: Everyone in Indian wealth is paid to touch the money. Choose your fee basis.

opts.t: A percentage of assets, like every incumbent
opts.fb: % AUM is the conflict you're supposed to be selling against — it can be inflated by pushing product and grows with assets, not effort. It also puts you in the same uneconomic math the big MFOs already lose at below ₹100 cr.

opts.t: A tiered flat retainer scaled to complexity, never asset size
opts.fb: Correct. ₹3–25 lakh tiered on geographies, entities and live succession — never on how much money is in the account. That is the mechanism that keeps the incentive clean and the fee legally uncapped.

decisions.q: 2 · How do you handle the Aamara conflict?
decisions.ctx: A PMS/AIF manager building a 'conflict-free' admin layer invites the charge that it just funnels clients into Aamara's own products.

opts.t: Verbal assurances + a gentle nudge toward Aamara's PMS
opts.fb: The moment the business soft-pushes Aamara, the entire conflict-free USP — the whole moat — dies. A soft firewall isn't a defence; it's the single biggest risk realised.

opts.t: A separate legal entity, arms-length, contractual no-cross-sell
opts.fb: Correct. The only credible defence is structural, not verbal: a separate company, product-agnostic mandate, written no-cross-sell charter. If a client independently picks Aamara's PMS, it's disclosed and at their own initiative.

decisions.q: 3 · How do you build the reporting engine?
decisions.ctx: Consolidated cross-asset reporting is the client's first 'wow.' Build it or buy it?

opts.t: Build a proprietary aggregation platform from scratch
opts.fb: Reporting engines are commoditised — a multi-year build burns capital re-inventing something you can license today. The value is the service layer on top, not the aggregation itself.

opts.t: License Asset Vantage, build a thin branded dashboard
opts.fb: Correct. Buy the India-focused, GL-integrated engine (cheaper than Addepar, already used by Indian FOs); reserve Addepar for the Premier tier; build only the thin client surface. Buy the engine, build the service.

decisions.ctx: You need break-even family density before you spread. Pick your beachhead.

opts.t: Go national from Day 1 to grab share fast
opts.fb: This is a people-heavy business that's margin-negative until ~50 families. Spreading thin nationally before break-even density burns the senior team's time and the runway with it.

opts.t: Mumbai + Bengaluru first, to break-even density
opts.fb: Correct. Mumbai holds 35.4% of India's UHNW base and Bengaluru is the founder/ESOP epicentre. Reach the ~75–90-family break-even before Delhi/Hyderabad/Pune, then NRI hubs.

human.titleHtml: The most important % in the deck.
human.lead: The moat isn't a spreadsheet — it's trust, and trust has a number. In a market where the majority of the people paid to advise you admit they mis-sell, being paid not to touch the money is both the ethics and the strategy.

rows.label: The conflicted world — bank RMs admitting mis-selling
The conflict-free world — annual client retention (EST.)

human.note: Banks earn 65–70% first-year commission on in-house insurance — the invisible bill the 'free' private-banking client never sees. Remove that conflict and switching means re-plumbing an entire financial life, so retention runs above 90% (EST.). In a category whose entire value is trust, the 57% is the opening and the 90% is the moat.

quiz.lead: Five questions. Then you cast the vote you'd cast in the room.

items.q: Why is the flat retainer legal and uncapped?
opts: Because family offices are exempt from all SEBI rules
Because the business does not recommend securities, so the RIA ₹1.51 lakh fixed-fee cap does not apply
Because it registers as an RIA and charges within the cap
items.e: The SEBI (Investment Advisers) Regulations govern 'investment advice in securities.' Pure administration, reporting, bill-pay and coordination are not that — so the ₹1.51 lakh cap binds only a registered adviser, not this entity.

items.q: What is the single biggest execution risk the dossier names?
opts: Not enough demand for the service
items.e: A PMS/AIF manager building a 'conflict-free' layer invites the charge that it funnels clients into Aamara products. The only credible defence is structural — separate entity, arms-length, contractual no-cross-sell — not verbal.

items.q: Roughly how many families does the business need to break even? (EST.)
items.e: At ~₹8 lakh average ACV and steady-state opex of ~₹5–6 cr, break-even is approximately 75–90 active families (EST.) — achievable within roughly 24–36 months given referral-led acquisition.

items.q: Why can't 360 ONE, Kotak or Nuvama simply copy this?
opts: The innovator's dilemma — they can't go flat-fee and product-agnostic without cannibalising the % AUM and distribution revenue that funds them
They are legally barred from admin work
items.e: They can bolt on a 'family-office desk,' but their institutional DNA is product distribution. Copying the positioning without changing the revenue model would be immediately visible to a skeptical client.

items.q: Which band does this business actually serve?
opts: The ₹25–200 cr 'emerging-affluent / lower-UHNI' band — top of the HNWI pool plus the affluent tier beneath it
items.e: The band sits below the US$30M UHNWI cutoff, so it can't be sized off the 19,877 number. It's too small for a bespoke single-family office yet too complex — and too product-pushed — for a private bank.

vote.prompt: You've seen the whitespace, the regulatory line, the personas, the unit economics and the trust number. What's your call?
vote.yesMsg: Backed. Your Phase-1 first move (per the Action Plan): inside the first 30 days, incorporate the separate legal entity and draft the arms-length / no-cross-sell charter versus Aamara, obtain a legal opinion confirming non-RIA status, and shortlist Asset Vantage versus Addepar. The firewall is existential — build it before the first family, not after.
vote.noMsg: Passed — and defensibly so. The dossier agrees the variance is real: a people-heavy cost base (60–70% personnel) that's margin-negative until ~50 families, a self-constructed bottom-up TAM rather than a comparable that's raised institutional capital, and an Aamara conflict-of-interest perception that lives or dies on a firewall holding. A pass here is a legitimate IC position — the case study never pretends otherwise.

footer.note1: This playable walk-through is a companion to the full institutional dossier — it simplifies for engagement and never overrides the source document.
footer.note2: Every number here is drawn verbatim from the dossier, including its EST. flags and unverified-source caveats.
